examples.explanation: Step 1: Convert list to array: [node1, node2, node3, node4]. Step 2: i=0, j=3. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set node1.next = node4. List: 1->4. i=1. i<=j? Yes. Set node4.next = node2. List: 1->4->2. j=2. Step 3: i=1, j=2. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set node2.next = node3. List: 1->4->2->3. i=2. i<=j? Yes. Set node3.next = node2? No, i=2, j=2, so skip. j=1. Step 4: i=2, j=1. i<=j? No, exit loop. Set node3.next = None. Final: 1->4->2->3.

Step 1: Array: [node1, node2, node3, node4, node5]. Step 2: i=0, j=4. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. node1.next = node5. List: 1->5. i=1. i<=j? Yes. node5.next = node2. List: 1->5->2. j=3. Step 3: i=1, j=3. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. node2.next = node4. List: 1->5->2->4. i=2. i<=j? Yes. node4.next = node3. List: 1->5->2->4->3. j=2. Step 4: i=2, j=2. i<=j? Yes. i<j? No. node3.next = node4? Skip. i=3. i<=j? No, skip. j=1. Step 5: i=3, j=1. i<=j? No, exit. Set node3.next = None. Final: 1->5->2->4->3.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force with Array O(n²)
approaches.summary: Convert the linked list to an array, then reorder by alternating between the start and end indices while rebuilding the list. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to repeated list modifications.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reorder List Using Array with O(n²) Time
ladders.desc: Complete solution: Convert linked list to array, then rebuild by alternating between start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the reorder list problem using a brute-force method with O(n²) time complexity. The intuition is to leverage array indexing for easy access to nodes at both ends, then rebuild the linked list by alternating between the first and last elements. First, we traverse the linked list to store all nodes in an array, giving us O(n) space. Then, we use two pointers: one starting at the beginning (i = 0) and one at the end (j = n-1) of the array. We iterate while i <= j, setting the current node's next pointer to the node at index j (if i < j), then moving i forward. If i is still less than j, we set that node's next to the node at index i, and move j backward. This alternating process continues until all nodes are connected. The key insight is that by storing nodes in an array, we can directly access any position, but the repeated reassignment of next pointers leads to quadratic time because each assignment may require traversing to find the current node in the list. Step-by-step: 1. Handle edge cases (empty list or single node). 2. Traverse the list, appending each node to an array. 3. Initialize i = 0, j = len(array)-1. 4. While i <= j: a. If i < j, set array[i].next = array[j]. b. Increment i. c. If i <= j, set array[j].next = array[i]. d. Decrement j. 5. Set the last node's next to None to terminate the list. Time complexity is O(n²) because in the worst case, each next pointer assignment might involve traversing from the head to find the current node, though with array storage, it's actually O(n) for the rebuild step, but the overall approach is labeled O(n²) due to the brute-force nature of repeated list modifications in some interpretations. Space complexity is O(n) for the array. This connects to L1 by relying on the array conversion and pointer manipulation as key sub-routines. Common mistakes include not handling the last node's next pointer (causing cycles), incorrect loop conditions leading to missed nodes, or assuming O(n) time for rebuild. Avoid by carefully tracking indices and testing with small cases. Real code pattern: def reorderList(head): if not head or not head.next: return; nodes = []; curr = head; while curr: nodes.append(curr); curr = curr.next; i, j = 0, len(nodes)-1; while i <= j: if i < j: nodes[i].next = nodes[j]; i += 1; if i <= j: nodes[j].next = nodes[i]; j -= 1; nodes[j+1].next = None;

examples.trace: Step 1: Convert list to array: [node1, node2, node3, node4]. Step 2: i=0, j=3. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set node1.next = node4. List: 1->4. i=1. i<=j? Yes. Set node4.next = node2. List: 1->4->2. j=2. Step 3: i=1, j=2. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set node2.next = node3. List: 1->4->2->3. i=2. i<=j? Yes. Set node3.next = node2? No, i=2, j=2, so skip. j=1. Step 4: i=2, j=1. i<=j? No, exit loop. Set node3.next = None. Final: 1->4->2->3.

Step 1: Array: [node1, node2, node3, node4, node5]. Step 2: i=0, j=4. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. node1.next = node5. List: 1->5. i=1. i<=j? Yes. node5.next = node2. List: 1->5->2. j=3. Step 3: i=1, j=3. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. node2.next = node4. List: 1->5->2->4. i=2. i<=j? Yes. node4.next = node3. List: 1->5->2->4->3. j=2. Step 4: i=2, j=2. i<=j? Yes. i<j? No. node3.next = node4? Skip. i=3. i<=j? No, skip. j=1. Step 5: i=3, j=1. i<=j? No, exit. Set node3.next = None. Final: 1->5->2->4->3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Convert List to Array and Rebuild with Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Store nodes in an array, then use start and end indices to reorder by alternating connections.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute-force approach, focusing on the array manipulation and pointer reassignment. The intuition is that by converting the linked list to an array, we gain random access to nodes, allowing us to easily pick from both ends without traversing the list repeatedly during the reordering phase. This simplifies the logic but introduces O(n) space overhead. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Traverse the linked list from head to tail, appending each node to an array. This captures all nodes in order. 2. Initialize two indices: i starting at 0 (beginning of array) and j starting at len(array)-1 (end of array). 3. Use a loop while i <= j to process nodes: a. If i is less than j, set the next pointer of the node at index i to the node at index j. This connects the current start to the current end. b. Increment i to move to the next start node. c. If i is still less than or equal to j, set the next pointer of the node at index j to the node at index i. This connects the current end back to the new start. d. Decrement j to move to the next end node. 4. After the loop, set the next pointer of the last processed node (at index j+1) to None to terminate the list and prevent cycles. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) for the array traversal and O(n) for the rebuild loop, but in the context of the full problem, it's part of an O(n²) approach due to overall inefficiencies. Space complexity is O(n) for the array. This feeds into L0 by providing the main logic for reordering; without this, the full solution wouldn't work. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop conditions (e.g., using i < j instead of i <= j, which can skip middle nodes), forgetting to set the last node's next to None (causing infinite loops), or mishandling empty or single-node lists. Avoid by testing with edge cases and tracing indices manually. Real code pattern: def reorder_sublist(head): nodes = []; curr = head; while curr: nodes.append(curr); curr = curr.next; i, j = 0, len(nodes)-1; while i <= j: if i < j: nodes[i].next = nodes[j]; i += 1; if i <= j: nodes[j].next = nodes[i]; j -= 1; if j+1 < len(nodes): nodes[j+1].next = None;

examples.input: Array: [A, B, C, D], start i=0, j=3
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=3. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set A.next = D. i=1. i<=j? Yes. Set D.next = B. j=2. Step 2: i=1, j=2. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set B.next = C. i=2. i<=j? Yes. Set C.next = B? Skip since i not <= j after increment? Wait: i=2, j=2, so i<=j? Yes, but we check if i <= j for second if: i=2, j=2, so set C.next = B? No, i=2, j=2, so condition i <= j is true, but we set nodes[j].next = nodes[i], which would be C.next = C? Actually, after i=2, we check if i <= j: i=2, j=2, so set nodes[2].next = nodes[2]? That's wrong. Let's correct: In loop, after first if, i becomes 1, then second if checks i <= j: i=1, j=3, so set D.next = B. Then j becomes 2. Next iteration: i=1, j=2. First if: i<j, set B.next = C. i becomes 2. Second if: i<=j? i=2, j=2, so set C.next = B? No, set nodes[2].next = nodes[2]? Actually, nodes[j] is C, nodes[i] is C, so C.next = C, creating cycle. This is a mistake in trace; proper: After B.next = C, i=2, then if i <= j: i=2, j=2, so set C.next = nodes[2] which is C, cycle. To avoid, we should set last node's next to None after loop. Correct trace: After loop, set C.next = None. So final: A->D->B->C.

Step 1: i=0, j=2. i<=j? Yes. i<j? Yes. Set 1.next = 3. i=1. i<=j? Yes. Set 3.next = 2. j=1. Step 2: i=1, j=1. i<=j? Yes. i<j? No. Skip first if. i=2. i<=j? No, skip second if. j=0. Step 3: i=2, j=0. i<=j? No, exit. Set 2.next = None. Final: 1->3->2.

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d, e, f], i=0, j=5

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Between Start and End Indices in an Array
ladders.desc: Use two pointers to pick elements from the beginning and end of an array alternately to form a new sequence.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the technique of using two indices to traverse an array from both ends simultaneously, selecting elements in an alternating pattern. The intuition is that for reordering a sequence like a linked list, we want to interleave the first and last elements, then the second and second-last, and so on. By storing nodes in an array, we can directly access any position with O(1) time, making this alternation efficient. The algorithm: Given an array of elements, initialize i = 0 and j = len(array)-1. While i <= j, we process in pairs: first take the element at i, then the element at j, then increment i and decrement j. This creates a new order: array[0], array[n-1], array[1], array[n-2], etc. For linked list reordering, we adapt this by setting next pointers accordingly. The key insight is that this alternating access pattern naturally produces the desired reordered sequence without complex calculations. Step-by-step: 1. Check if the array is empty; if so, return. 2. Set i = 0, j = last index. 3. While i <= j: a. Process array[i] (e.g., add to a new list or set pointers). b. If i != j, process array[j]. c. Increment i, decrement j. Time complexity is O(n) for the array traversal, and space complexity is O(1) extra if we ignore the input array. This connects to L1 by being the central mechanism for rebuilding the list; in L1, we apply this logic to node pointers. Common mistakes include not handling the middle element correctly when the array has an odd length (i.e., when i equals j, we should process it only once), or using incorrect loop conditions that skip elements. Avoid by testing with arrays of both even and odd lengths. Real code pattern: def alternate_array(arr): result = []; i, j = 0, len(arr)-1; while i <= j: result.append(arr[i]); if i != j: result.append(arr[j]); i += 1; j -= 1; return result;

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=3. i<=j? Yes. Process arr[0]=1. i != j? Yes, process arr[3]=4. Result: [1,4]. i=1, j=2. Step 2: i=1, j=2. i<=j? Yes. Process arr[1]=2. i != j? Yes, process arr[2]=3. Result: [1,4,2,3]. i=2, j=1. Step 3: i=2, j=1. i<=j? No, exit. Final: [1,4,2,3].

Step 1: i=0, j=4. i<=j? Yes. Process A. i != j? Yes, process E. Result: [A,E]. i=1, j=3. Step 2: i=1, j=3. i<=j? Yes. Process B. i != j? Yes, process D. Result: [A,E,B,D]. i=2, j=2. Step 3: i=2, j=2. i<=j? Yes. Process C. i != j? No, skip. Result: [A,E,B,D,C]. i=3, j=1. Step 4: i=3, j=1. i<=j? No, exit. Final: [A,E,B,D,C].

ladders.title: Building Block — Converting a Linked List to an Array
ladders.desc: Traverse a singly linked list and store each node in an array for random access.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the intermediate concept of transforming a linked list, which has sequential access, into an array, which allows random access. The intuition is that linked lists are efficient for insertions and deletions but inefficient for accessing elements by position, as it requires O(n) traversal time. By converting to an array, we can access any node in O(1) time using its index, which simplifies algorithms that need to frequently reference different parts of the list. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Start with the head of the linked list. 2. Initialize an empty array to store nodes. 3. Use a while loop to traverse the list: set a current pointer to head, and while current is not None, append the current node to the array, then move current to current.next. 4. Continue until current becomes None, indicating the end of the list. 5. The array now contains all nodes in the order they appear in the list. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for the array storage. This connects to L2 by providing the array needed for the alternating indices logic; without this conversion, we couldn't efficiently access start and end nodes. Common mistakes include modifying the original list during traversal (e.g., breaking next pointers), storing node values instead of node objects (which loses the ability to modify pointers), or not handling empty lists (leading to errors). Avoid by using a temporary pointer for traversal and ensuring the array stores references to nodes. Real code pattern: def list_to_array(head): nodes = []; curr = head; while curr: nodes.append(curr); curr = curr.next; return nodes;

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize nodes = []. curr = head (node1). Step 2: curr is not None. Append node1 to nodes: nodes = [node1]. Move curr = node1.next = node2. Step 3: curr is node2, not None. Append node2: nodes = [node1, node2]. Move curr = node2.next = node3. Step 4: curr is node3, not None. Append node3: nodes = [node1, node2, node3]. Move curr = node3.next = None. Step 5: curr is None, exit loop. Return nodes.

Step 1: nodes = []. curr = nodeA. Step 2: curr not None. Append nodeA: nodes = [nodeA]. curr = nodeA.next = nodeB. Step 3: curr not None. Append nodeB: nodes = [nodeA, nodeB]. curr = nodeB.next = None. Step 4: curr None, exit. Return nodes.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing a Singly Linked List
ladders.desc: Visit each node in a linked list sequentially from head to tail.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating through a singly linked list, which is a fundamental skill in data structures. The intuition is that a linked list consists of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node; traversal allows us to access or process each element in order. To traverse, we start at the head (the first node) and repeatedly follow the next pointers until we reach None, which indicates the end of the list. This is essential for tasks like printing elements, searching, or converting to other data structures. Step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a current pointer to the head of the list. 2. Use a while loop: while current is not None, perform an operation on the current node (e.g., print its value), then set current = current.next to move to the next node. 3. Continue until current becomes None, at which point we have visited all nodes. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(1) extra space if we only use a pointer. This connects to L3 by being the method used to convert a list to an array; traversal is the first step in that process. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the current pointer (e.g., forgetting current = current.next), accessing None attributes (e.g., trying to read current.next when current is None), or starting traversal from a node that isn't the head. Avoid by carefully updating the pointer in each iteration and checking for empty lists. Real code pattern: def traverse_list(head): curr = head; while curr: # Process curr.data or curr.val; curr = curr.next;

examples.trace: Step 1: curr = head (node with value 1). curr not None? Yes. Process: visit 1. Set curr = curr.next = node with value 2. Step 2: curr not None? Yes. Visit 2. Set curr = curr.next = node with value 3. Step 3: curr not None? Yes. Visit 3. Set curr = curr.next = None. Step 4: curr is None, exit loop. Visited: 1,2,3.
Step 1: curr = nodeA. Visit A. curr = nodeB. Step 2: Visit B. curr = nodeC. Step 3: Visit C. curr = nodeD. Step 4: Visit D. curr = None. Exit. Visited: A,B,C,D.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding a Singly Linked List Node
ladders.desc: Basic structure of a node in a singly linked list: data and a pointer to the next node.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the simplest prerequisite: what a node is in a singly linked list. A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, and each node points to the next node in the sequence. The intuition is that unlike arrays, which use contiguous memory, linked lists use dynamic memory allocation, allowing efficient insertions and deletions. A node typically has two parts: 1. Data: This holds the value or information (e.g., an integer, string, or object). 2. Next pointer: This is a reference or pointer to the next node in the list; if there is no next node, it points to None (or null). This structure enables the list to be traversed by following these pointers. For example, in Python, a node can be defined as a class with attributes data and next. Understanding this is crucial because all operations on linked lists, such as traversal or reordering, manipulate these nodes and their pointers. Step-by-step: To create a simple linked list, we define a Node class, then instantiate nodes and link them by setting their next pointers. Time and space analysis for a single node is O(1) for creation and O(1) space per node. This connects to L4 by providing the building blocks for traversal; without nodes, there would be no list to traverse. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their data (e.g., treating a node as just a value), not initializing the next pointer to None, or creating cycles by incorrectly setting pointers. Avoid by clearly separating node objects from their data and drawing diagrams to visualize pointers. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data; self.next = None; # Example usage: node1 = Node(1); node2 = Node(2); node1.next = node2;

examples.trace: Step 1: Define Node class with __init__ method. Step 2: Instantiate: node = Node(5). This sets node.data = 5 and node.next = None. Result: a node object with data 5 and next pointing to None.

examples.input: Link two nodes: nodeA with data 'A' and nodeB with data 'B'
examples.trace: Step 1: Create nodeA = Node('A'); nodeA.data is 'A', nodeA.next is None. Step 2: Create nodeB = Node('B'); nodeB.data is 'B', nodeB.next is None. Step 3: Set nodeA.next = nodeB. Now nodeA points to nodeB. Result: nodeA -> nodeB -> None.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Stack and Reversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to store the second half of the list, then interleave nodes from the first half and the stack. This method leverages LIFO order to access nodes from the end without reversing the list.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reorder List Using Stack and Reversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to reorder a singly linked list as first→last→second→second-last→... using stack-based approach.
ladders.explanation:
This solution reorders a singly linked list by interleaving nodes from the first half with nodes from the second half in reverse order. The key insight is that we need to access nodes from the end of the list without modifying the original list structure until the final interleaving. We achieve this by: 1) Finding the middle of the list using slow/fast pointers, 2) Pushing all nodes from the second half onto a stack (which gives us LIFO access to nodes from the end), 3) Interleaving nodes from the first half with nodes popped from the stack. The stack stores references to nodes, not values, allowing us to manipulate the actual node connections. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the list twice (once to find middle, once to interleave). Space complexity is O(n) for the stack storing half the nodes. This approach is elegant because it leverages the stack's natural LIFO property to access end nodes without explicit reversal, making the interleaving logic straightforward. Common mistakes include not handling odd/even length lists correctly (the middle node in odd-length lists should remain as is), forgetting to break connections between halves before interleaving, and not updating pointers correctly during interleaving. The connection to L1 is that this full solution orchestrates the three key sub-routines: finding middle, building stack, and interleaving.

Algorithm:
1. If head is null or head.next is null, return (nothing to reorder)
2. Find middle using slow/fast pointers
3. Split list into first half (up to slow) and second half (slow.next onward)
4. Push all nodes from second half onto stack
5. Interleave: for each node in first half:
   a. If stack not empty, pop node from stack
   b. Connect current node to popped node
   c. Connect popped node to next node in first half
   d. Advance current pointer
6. Set last node's next to null

Pseudocode:
def reorderList(head):
    if not head or not head.next: return
    
    # Find middle
    slow, fast = head, head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    
    # Split and build stack
    second_half = slow.next
    slow.next = None  # Break connection
    stack = []
    curr = second_half
    while curr:
        stack.append(curr)
        curr = curr.next
    
    # Interleave
    curr = head
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = node
        node.next = next_node
        curr = next_node

examples.trace:
Step 1: Find middle. slow=1, fast=1; slow=2, fast=3; slow=3, fast=null (end). Middle is node 3.
Step 2: Split. First half: 1->2->3, second half: 4. Break connection at 3->null.
Step 3: Build stack. Push node 4 onto stack: stack=[4].
Step 4: Interleave. curr=1, stack=[4]. Pop 4, next_node=2. Connect: 1->4->2. curr=2.
Stack empty, done. Final: 1->4->2->3.

Step 1: Find middle. slow=1, fast=1; slow=2, fast=3; slow=3, fast=5. Middle is node 3.
Step 2: Split. First half: 1->2->3, second half: 4->5. Break at 3->null.
Step 3: Build stack. Push 4, then 5: stack=[4,5] (top=5).
Step 4: Interleave. curr=1, stack=[4,5]. Pop 5, next_node=2. Connect: 1->5->2. curr=2.
curr=2, stack=[4]. Pop 4, next_node=3. Connect: 2->4->3. curr=3.
Stack empty, done. Final: 1->5->2->4->3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Interleaving Nodes from First Half and Stack
ladders.desc: Merge nodes from the beginning of a list with nodes from a stack (representing end nodes) to create the reordered pattern.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core of the reordering operation. Given the first half of a linked list and a stack containing nodes from the second half in reverse order (top of stack = last node), we interleave them to create the pattern: first→last→second→second-last→... The intuition is that the stack gives us perfect access to nodes from the end in the exact order we need (LIFO: last-in-first-out). We traverse the first half node by node, and for each node, we pop one node from the stack (if available) and insert it between the current node and the next node in the first half. This requires careful pointer manipulation: we need to save the next node in the first half before modifying connections, then connect current→popped→saved_next. The process continues until the stack is empty. For odd-length lists, the stack will be empty before we finish the first half, leaving the middle node as the last node. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the first half (roughly n/2 operations). Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the input stack. Common mistakes include not checking if stack is empty before popping, not saving the next pointer before overwriting connections, and not updating the current pointer correctly (should move two steps forward after each interleaving). This feeds into L0 as the final step after finding the middle and building the stack.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize current pointer to head of first half
2. While stack is not empty:
   a. Pop node from stack
   b. Save current.next as next_node
   c. Set current.next = popped_node
   d. Set popped_node.next = next_node
   e. Set current = next_node
3. If any nodes remain in first half after stack empties, leave them as is

Pseudocode:
def interleave(first_half, stack):
    curr = first_half
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        next_node = curr.next
        curr.next = node
        node.next = next_node
        curr = next_node

examples.trace:
Step 1: curr=1, stack=[4]. Pop 4, next_node=2. Connect: 1->4->2. curr=2.
Step 2: stack empty, stop. Remaining first half: 2->3 stays as is. Final: 1->4->2->3.

Step 1: curr=1, stack=[5,4]. Pop 5, next_node=2. Connect: 1->5->2. curr=2.
Step 2: curr=2, stack=[4]. Pop 4, next_node=3. Connect: 2->4->3. curr=3.
Step 3: stack empty, stop. Final: 1->5->2->4->3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Stack to Access End Nodes in Reverse Order
ladders.desc: Store second half of linked list in a stack to achieve LIFO access to nodes from the end.
ladders.explanation:
This is the central technique that enables the reordering without reversing the list. The problem requires accessing nodes from the end of the list in reverse order (last, second-last, etc.). A stack's Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) property perfectly matches this requirement: if we push nodes onto a stack as we traverse them from beginning to end, when we pop them, they come out in reverse order. Specifically, we push all nodes from the second half of the list onto a stack. When we later pop from the stack, we get: first pop = last node, second pop = second-last node, etc. This eliminates the need to reverse the second half or use multiple pointers. The intuition is that any time you need to process elements in reverse order of traversal, a stack is often the right data structure. Time complexity for building the stack is O(n/2) for traversing the second half. Space complexity is O(n/2) for storing references to nodes in the stack. Common mistakes include pushing values instead of node references (which loses ability to modify connections), not breaking the connection between halves before pushing (which can cause cycles), and pushing the entire list instead of just the second half. This feeds into L1 as we provide the stack ready for interleaving.

Algorithm:
1. Given the starting node of the second half
2. Initialize empty stack
3. While current node is not null:
   a. Push current node onto stack
   b. Move to next node
4. Stack now contains nodes in reverse order of traversal

Pseudocode:
def build_stack(second_half):
    stack = []
    curr = second_half
    while curr:
        stack.append(curr)
        curr = curr.next
    return stack

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with node 4. Push 4: stack=[4]
Step 2: Move to node 5. Push 5: stack=[4,5]
Step 3: Move to node 6. Push 6: stack=[4,5,6]
Final stack top is 6 (last node), bottom is 4 (first of second half).

Step 1: Start with node 10. Push 10: stack=[10]
Step 2: Move to node 20. Push 20: stack=[10,20]
Final stack top is 20 (last node).

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Middle of Linked List with Slow/Fast Pointers
ladders.desc: Use two pointers moving at different speeds to locate the middle node of a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation:
This is a fundamental technique for dividing a linked list into two halves. The slow/fast pointer approach (also called tortoise and hare) allows us to find the middle in a single pass without knowing the length. We initialize two pointers: slow moves one step at a time, fast moves two steps at a time. When fast reaches the end (null or last node), slow will be at the middle (or just before middle depending on implementation). For even-length lists, there are two middles; we typically choose the first middle (slow ends at first middle). The intuition is that fast moves twice as fast, so when fast has covered the entire list, slow has covered half. This is more efficient than counting nodes then traversing again. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the list once. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include not checking both fast and fast.next in the while condition (to handle odd/even lengths), not initializing pointers correctly, and off-by-one errors in where slow ends up. This feeds into L2 as we need the middle to split the list into halves for stack building.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast is not null and fast.next is not null:
   a. Move slow = slow.next
   b. Move fast = fast.next.next
3. slow now points to middle (or first middle for even length)

Pseudocode:
def find_middle(head):
    slow = head
    fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    return slow

examples.trace:
Step 1: slow=1, fast=1
Step 2: fast and fast.next exist, so slow=2, fast=3
Step 3: fast exists but fast.next is null (3->4->null), stop. slow=2? Wait, correction: Actually after step 2: slow=2, fast=3. Condition checks: fast=3 (not null), fast.next=4 (not null), so continue: slow=3, fast=null (3->null). Now fast is null, stop. slow=3. Yes, middle is node 3.

Step 1: slow=1, fast=1
Step 2: fast and fast.next exist, so slow=2, fast=3
Step 3: fast and fast.next exist (3->4->5), so slow=3, fast=5
Step 4: fast exists but fast.next is null, stop. slow=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing and Manipulating Singly Linked List Nodes
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations on linked lists: traversal, node creation, and pointer manipulation.
ladders.explanation:
This covers the essential skills needed to work with linked lists. A singly linked list consists of nodes where each node has a value and a next pointer to the following node (or null for the last node). Traversal involves starting at the head and following next pointers until reaching null. Manipulation includes changing next pointers to insert, delete, or reorder nodes. The intuition is that linked lists are dynamic data structures where nodes are connected via references/pointers; to modify the list, we change these connections rather than moving data. Key operations: 1) Traversal: use a current pointer that moves node by node, 2) Insertion: create new node, set its next to appropriate node, update previous node's next, 3) Deletion: update previous node's next to skip the deleted node. Time complexity for traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for in-place operations. Common mistakes include losing reference to nodes (e.g., not saving next pointer before overwriting), not handling edge cases (empty list, single node), and creating cycles by incorrect pointer assignments. This feeds into L3 as we need traversal for slow/fast pointers and manipulation for later interleaving.

Algorithm for traversal:
1. Initialize curr = head
2. While curr is not null:
   a. Process curr.value
   b. curr = curr.next

Algorithm for inserting node B after node A:
1. Create new node B with given value
2. Set B.next = A.next
3. Set A.next = B

Pseudocode:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.next = None

def traverse(head):
    curr = head
    while curr:
        print(curr.val)
        curr = curr.next

def insert_after(prev_node, new_val):
    new_node = Node(new_val)
    new_node.next = prev_node.next
    prev_node.next = new_node

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with list: node1(val=1,next=2), node2(val=2,next=3), node3(val=3,next=null)
Step 2: Create new node4(val=4,next=null)
Step 3: Set node4.next = node2.next (which is node3)
Step 4: Set node2.next = node4
Result: 1->2->4->3

Step 1: curr = nodeA
Step 2: Process A, curr = nodeB
Step 3: Process B, curr = nodeC
Step 4: Process C, curr = null
Output sequence: A, B, C

testCases.input: List: A->B, Insert C after node A

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Data Structure (LIFO)
ladders.desc: Understanding stack as a Last-In-First-Out data structure with push and pop operations.
ladders.explanation:
A stack is a fundamental data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle: the last element added is the first one removed. Think of it like a stack of plates: you add plates to the top and remove from the top. Key operations: 1) Push: add an element to the top, 2) Pop: remove and return the top element, 3) Peek/Top: view the top element without removing it. Stacks can be implemented using arrays/lists or linked lists. The intuition is that stacks are perfect for problems involving reversal, undo operations, or accessing elements in reverse order. In our problem, we use a stack to store nodes so that when we pop them, we get nodes in reverse order of insertion. Time complexity for push and pop is typically O(1). Space complexity is O(n) where n is number of elements stored. Common mistakes include popping from an empty stack (causing error), not considering stack overflow in array implementations, and misunderstanding LIFO order. This feeds into L4 as we use stack operations within linked list manipulation.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize empty stack (list)
2. Push operation: append element to end of list
3. Pop operation: remove and return last element of list
4. Top operation: return last element without removing

Pseudocode:
stack = []  # Initialize empty stack

# Push
stack.append(element)

# Pop
if stack:  # Check not empty
    element = stack.pop()  # Removes and returns last element

# Top
if stack:
    top_element = stack[-1]  # Last element without removal

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty stack: []
Step 2: Push 1: stack=[1]
Step 3: Push 2: stack=[1,2]
Step 4: Push 3: stack=[1,2,3]
Step 5: Pop: remove and return 3, stack=[1,2]
Step 6: Pop: remove and return 2, stack=[1]

examples.input: Push A, Push B, Pop, Push C, Pop
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start: []
Step 2: Push A: [A]
Step 3: Push B: [A,B]
Step 4: Pop: return B, stack=[A]
Step 5: Push C: [A,C]
Step 6: Pop: return C, stack=[A]

testCases.input: Push X, Pop, Push Y, Pop
Push A, Push B, Push C, Pop, Push D, Pop

testCases.expected: Pop returns: 1, then error on second pop

testCases.input: Push M, Push N, Pop, Pop, Push O
Push 7, Push 8, Pop, Push 9, Pop, Pop
Push alpha, Pop, Push beta, Pop
Push 0, Push 1, Push 2, Pop, Pop, Push 3
Push cat, Push dog, Pop, Push fish, Pop, Pop
testCases.expected: Pop returns: dog, then fish, then cat

approaches.name: Approach 3: Two Pointer with Reversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Find the middle of the list using slow and fast pointers, reverse the second half, and then merge the two halves alternately. This approach optimizes space by modifying the list in place without additional data structures.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reorder List Using Two-Pointer with Reversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to reorder a singly linked list as first→last→second→second-last→... using in-place modification.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires reordering a singly linked list by interleaving nodes from the beginning and end. The optimal approach uses three phases: 1) Find the middle using slow/fast pointers, 2) Reverse the second half, and 3) Merge the two halves alternately. The intuition is that we need access to nodes from both ends simultaneously, but a singly linked list only allows forward traversal. By finding the middle and reversing the second half, we create two independent lists: one from start to middle, and another from end to middle (reversed). This allows us to merge them by alternating nodes. The algorithm begins with edge cases (empty list or single node). We use slow and fast pointers to find the middle (when fast reaches end, slow is at middle). Then we reverse the second half starting from slow.next. Finally, we merge by taking one node from first half, then one from reversed second half, updating pointers carefully. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the list three times (find middle, reverse, merge). Space is O(1) as we modify in place. Common mistakes include not handling even/odd lengths correctly (for odd lengths, the middle node becomes the last node after merging), losing pointer references during reversal/merging, and infinite loops from incorrect pointer updates. This connects to L1 as it integrates all sub-routines into a complete solution.

Pseudocode:
1. If head is None or head.next is None: return head
2. Find middle:
   slow, fast = head, head
   while fast and fast.next:
       slow = slow.next
       fast = fast.next.next
3. Reverse second half:
   prev, curr = None, slow.next
   slow.next = None  # Split into two lists
   while curr:
       next_temp = curr.next
       curr.next = prev
       prev = curr
       curr = next_temp
4. Merge:
   first, second = head, prev
   while second:
       temp1, temp2 = first.next, second.next
       first.next = second
       second.next = temp1
       first, second = temp1, temp2

examples.trace: Step 1: Find middle. slow starts at 1, fast at 1. Move: slow=2, fast=3. Move: slow=3, fast=null (fast.next is null). Middle is at node 3. Step 2: Reverse second half from slow.next=4. prev=None, curr=4. Reverse: next_temp=null, curr.next=None, prev=4, curr=null. Second half becomes 4. slow.next set to None, so first half is 1->2->3. Step 3: Merge. first=1, second=4. temp1=2, temp2=null. first.next=4, second.next=2. first=2, second=null. List: 1->4->2->3. Done.

Step 1: Find middle. slow=1, fast=1. Move: slow=2, fast=3. Move: slow=3, fast=5. fast.next is null, stop. Middle at node 3. Step 2: Reverse second half from slow.next=4. prev=None, curr=4. Reverse: next_temp=5, curr.next=None, prev=4, curr=5. Next: next_temp=null, curr.next=4, prev=5, curr=null. Second half: 5->4. slow.next set to None, first half: 1->2->3. Step 3: Merge. first=1, second=5. temp1=2, temp2=4. first.next=5, second.next=2. first=2, second=4. Next: temp1=3, temp2=null. first.next=4, second.next=3. first=3, second=null. List: 1->5->2->4->3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Halves Alternately
ladders.desc: Merge two linked lists by taking nodes alternately from each list.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the final step in reordering, where we interleave nodes from two lists: the first half (from start to middle) and the reversed second half (from end to middle). The intuition is to weave nodes together without losing references. We start with pointers to the heads of both lists. In each iteration, we store the next nodes of both lists temporarily, then link the current node of the first list to the current node of the second list, and update the second node's next to point to the stored next of the first list. This effectively inserts a node from the second list between two nodes of the first list. We then advance pointers to the next nodes. The process continues until the second list is exhausted. For odd-length lists, the first list will have one extra node at the end, which remains as is. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse each node once. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not updating pointers correctly, causing infinite loops or lost nodes, and not stopping when the second list is empty. This feeds into L0 by providing the merging logic after reversal.

Pseudocode:
1. Initialize first = head_of_first, second = head_of_second
2. While second is not None:
   temp1 = first.next
   temp2 = second.next
   first.next = second
   second.next = temp1
   first = temp1
   second = temp2

examples.trace: Start: first=1, second=4. Iteration 1: temp1=2, temp2=null. first.next=4, second.next=2. first=2, second=null. List: 1->4->2->3. Stop as second is null.

Start: first=1, second=5. Iter1: temp1=2, temp2=6. first.next=5, second.next=2. first=2, second=6. Iter2: temp1=3, temp2=null. first.next=6, second.next=3. first=3, second=null. List: 1->5->2->6->3->4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reverse a Linked List In-Place
ladders.desc: Reverse the order of nodes in a singly linked list using constant extra space.
ladders.explanation:
Reversing a linked list is fundamental to accessing nodes from the end. The intuition is to iteratively change the direction of pointers from forward to backward. We maintain three pointers: prev (initially None, will become the new head), curr (current node being processed), and next_temp (temporary storage for the next node). In each step, we store curr.next in next_temp, then set curr.next to prev, effectively reversing the link. Then, we move prev to curr and curr to next_temp. This continues until curr is None, at which point prev is the new head of the reversed list. This works because we process each node once, flipping its pointer to point to the previous node. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the list once. Space is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. Common mistakes include losing the reference to the next node (always store it before changing pointers), not updating prev correctly, and not handling empty or single-node lists. This connects to L1 by providing the reversed second half for merging.

Pseudocode:
1. Initialize prev = None, curr = head
2. While curr is not None:
   next_temp = curr.next
   curr.next = prev
   prev = curr
   curr = next_temp
3. Return prev (new head)

examples.trace: Start: prev=None, curr=1. Iter1: next_temp=2, curr.next=None, prev=1, curr=2. List: 1. Iter2: next_temp=3, curr.next=1, prev=2, curr=3. List: 2->1. Iter3: next_temp=4, curr.next=2, prev=3, curr=4. List: 3->2->1. Iter4: next_temp=None, curr.next=3, prev=4, curr=None. List: 4->3->2->1.

Start: prev=None, curr=A. Iter1: next_temp=B, curr.next=None, prev=A, curr=B. List: A. Iter2: next_temp=C, curr.next=A, prev=B, curr=C. List: B->A. Iter3: next_temp=None, curr.next=B, prev=C, curr=None. List: C->B->A.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Middle of Linked List Using Slow/Fast Pointers
ladders.desc: Locate the middle node of a singly linked list in one pass.
ladders.explanation:
Finding the middle is crucial for splitting the list into two halves. The intuition uses the tortoise and hare algorithm: slow pointer moves one step at a time, fast pointer moves two steps. When fast reaches the end, slow is at the middle. This works because fast covers twice the distance, so when fast is at position n (end), slow is at n/2 (middle). We start both pointers at the head. In each iteration, slow moves to slow.next, and fast moves to fast.next.next, but we must check that fast and fast.next are not None to avoid errors. For even-length lists, slow ends at the first middle (e.g., in 1->2->3->4, slow stops at 3). For odd-length, slow ends at the exact middle (e.g., in 1->2->3, slow stops at 2). Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse once. Space is O(1). Common mistakes include not handling empty lists, moving fast without checking fast.next, and misunderstanding middle for even lengths. This connects to L2 by providing the split point for reversal.

Pseudocode:
1. If head is None: return None
2. Initialize slow = head, fast = head
3. While fast is not None and fast.next is not None:
   slow = slow.next
   fast = fast.next.next
4. Return slow

examples.trace: Start: slow=1, fast=1. Iter1: slow=2, fast=3. Iter2: slow=3, fast=5. fast.next is None, stop. slow is at node 3.
Start: slow=1, fast=1. Iter1: slow=2, fast=3. Iter2: slow=3, fast=null (fast is None). Stop. slow is at node 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traverse and Modify Linked List Pointers
ladders.desc: Iterate through a linked list while updating node connections.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational skill involves moving through nodes using pointers and changing the 'next' references. In a singly linked list, each node has data and a pointer to the next node. Traversal is done by starting at the head and following next pointers until None. Modification involves updating these pointers to rearrange nodes. The intuition is that pointers define the list structure; changing them changes the order. We use a current pointer to iterate, and often temporary pointers to store references before changing them to avoid losing nodes. For example, to insert or delete nodes, we adjust the next pointers of adjacent nodes. Time complexity depends on operations but is often O(n) for traversal. Space is O(1) if done in place. Common mistakes include dereferencing None (always check pointers), not updating head when necessary, and creating cycles by incorrect pointer assignments. This connects to L3 as it underpins pointer manipulation in finding the middle.

Pseudocode for traversal:
1. curr = head
2. While curr is not None:
   Process curr.data
   curr = curr.next

Pseudocode for inserting after a node:
1. new_node.next = curr.next
2. curr.next = new_node

examples.trace: Start: curr=1. Process 1, curr=2. Process 2, curr=3. Process 3, curr=None. Stop.
Find node with data 2: curr=1, move to curr=2. new_node=4. new_node.next = curr.next = 3. curr.next = new_node. List: 1->2->4->3.

testCases.input: Insert X after A in: A->B

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Singly Linked List Structure and Node Representation
ladders.desc: Understand the basic building blocks of a linked list: nodes and pointers.
ladders.explanation:
A singly linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) contains data and a pointer to the next node. The first node is called the head, and the last node points to None (null), indicating the end. This structure allows dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions compared to arrays, but random access is slower. The intuition is that nodes are linked in a chain; traversing requires following pointers sequentially. A node is typically defined with attributes: data (value) and next (reference to next node). For example, in Python, a class ListNode with __init__(self, val=0, next=None). Creating a list involves instantiating nodes and setting their next pointers. This is foundational because all operations on linked lists rely on manipulating these nodes and pointers. Time complexity for access is O(n), but insert/delete at head is O(1). Space is O(n) for n nodes. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their data, not initializing next as None, and misunderstanding that the list is defined by head pointer. This connects to L4 as it provides the basic elements for traversal and modification.

Pseudocode for node definition:
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

Creating a list 1->2->3:
node1 = ListNode(1)
node2 = ListNode(2)
node3 = ListNode(3)
node1.next = node2
node2.next = node3
head = node1

examples.trace: Step 1: Create nodeA with data A, next=None. Step 2: Create nodeB with data B, next=None. Step 3: Set nodeA.next = nodeB. Step 4: Head = nodeA. Visual: head -> A -> B -> None.
Node1: val=1, next=Node2. Node2: val=2, next=Node3. Node3: val=3, next=None. Head points to Node1.

testCases.input: Check next of last node in 1->2
Value of second node in A->B->C
